lesson.menu_desc: Align a divided room without steamrolling anyone: acknowledge both sides fairly, name the common ground everyone shares, propose a way forward with hypothetical language (what if we / suppose we / I'd suggest), build on others' points instead of overriding them, and close by checking everyone can live with the decision — turning a stalemate into a workable win-win — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 15 — Building Consensus — Aligning a Divided Room
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 15 — Building Consensus

listenings.text: Right, the leadership meeting is in twenty minutes and the room is split down the middle. Operations wants to protect this year's margin; growth wants to fund the new regions now. Two factions, both with a point, and if I just push my side we end in a stalemate. So here is my plan to broker it. First, I acknowledge both sides honestly before I argue anything. Operations is right that the margin matters, and growth is right that we cannot stall. If people feel heard, they give ground; if they feel run over, they dig in. Second, I name the common ground out loud, because there always is some. We all want the company healthy in eighteen months, not just this quarter. Starting from what we share lowers the temperature. Third, I propose with hypothetical language, not orders. What if we funded one region now and reviewed it at the half-year? Suppose we capped the spend so the margin holds. That invites people in instead of forcing a yes. Fourth, I build on whatever good point someone makes rather than overriding it. And last, before we move on, I check that everyone can genuinely live with it, not a lukewarm shrug but a real yes. A decision nobody owns falls apart by Friday. Acknowledge, find the middle ground, propose softly, and confirm. That is how you turn a divided room into a win-win.

Hi Victor, it's Adriana. A quick word before you walk into that leadership meeting, because aligning a divided room is a real skill and most people get it wrong. Three things. First, do not start by selling your answer. Start by making both factions feel understood. Say it plainly: there's merit in both positions. The moment people feel heard, they stop defending and start listening, and they will concede far more than you expect. Skip this and the room stays in a stalemate, no matter how good your idea is. Second, build the decision from the common ground up, not from your conclusion down. Name what everyone already agrees on, then move toward the middle ground from there. People reconcile around shared goals, not around your slide. Third, and this is where strong managers slip, propose tentatively and then check for real buy-in. Use what if and suppose and I'd suggest, so it feels like a shared idea, not a verdict. Then close the loop: can everyone live with this? If you only get a lukewarm nod, you do not have consensus, you have a problem that comes back next week. A real yes from the whole room is worth more than a brilliant plan half the room resents. Acknowledge, converge, propose, confirm. You will get them there.
